note_data.title: Chemical formulae and equations
keyPoints: Compound - 2 or more elements chemically joined together - Contains Covalent or Ionic Bonds
Mixtures - 2 or more substances which are not chemically joined together - Can consist of Elements, Compounds, or Elements + Compounds
Pure Substances have: Fixed composition, Fixed Mp/Bp, Produces only 1 spot on chromatogram, All molecules same (equal)
Mixtures have: Variable Composition, Variable Bp/Mp, 2 or more spots on Chromatogram, 2 or more diff. molecules
Solid & Liquid solution → Crystallization (Obtaining solid) – Solute
Solid & Liquid solution → Distillation (Obtaining Liquid) – Solvent
Mixture of Organic Compounds → Chromatography (Eg. Colour Dyes)

definitions.definition: 2 or more elements chemically joined together - Contains Covalent or Ionic Bonds
2 or more substances which are not chemically joined together
no more solid can dissolve in it

howToAnswer.instruction: Give the chemical formula or equation exactly as required with correct symbols and numbers
Add coefficients to make equal numbers of each atom type on both sides
Give the systematic chemical name following naming rules
Give the information requested clearly in one sentence

modelAnswer.question: Write a balanced chemical equation for the reaction between magnesium and hydrochloric acid to produce magnesium chloride and hydrogen gas. Include state symbols.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Correct formulae for all reactants and products

commonMistakes: Confusing compounds (chemically joined) with mixtures (not chemically joined)
Not understanding that pure substances have fixed composition while mixtures have variable composition
Mixing up filtration methods - using wrong separation technique for different mixture types
examinerTips: Any question that wants the method to obtain (make) crystals, the process will be 'Crystallisation'
The constant temperature of distillation process = Boiling Point of pure liquid
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: No specific diagram required, but students should be able to represent particle models showing conservation of atoms in chemical reactions
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q2 (3 marks) — they asked students to write balanced chemical equations for the reaction between magnesium and hydrochloric acid, including state symbols
N/D 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) tested formula writing by asking for the chemical formula of calcium hydroxide
M/J 2022 P2 Q4(b) (4 marks) — students had to balance the equation for combustion of propane and calculate the mass of carbon dioxide produced
N/D 2023 P2 Q1(c) (2 marks) asked students to write the ionic equation for the precipitation of silver chloride from silver nitrate and sodium chloride solutions

workedExample.question: Aluminum reacts with copper(II) sulfate solution. Write a balanced chemical equation for this reaction, including state symbols. (3 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify reactants and products. Aluminum displaces copper from copper(II) sulfate, forming aluminum sulfate and copper metal
Step 2: Write unbalanced equation with correct formulae: Al + CuSO₄ → Al₂(SO₄)₃ + Cu
Step 3: Balance the equation by counting atoms. Need 2 Al atoms and 3 CuSO₄ units: 2Al + 3CuSO₄ → Al₂(SO₄)₃ + 3Cu
Step 4: Add state symbols: Al is solid (s), CuSO₄ is aqueous (aq), Al₂(SO₄)₃ is aqueous (aq), Cu is solid (s)
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct formulae, 1 mark for balanced equation, 1 mark for correct state symbols

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates wrote incorrect formulae for ionic compounds' — always check that charges balance when writing formulae like Ca(OH)₂ or Al₂(SO₄)₃
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently omitted state symbols or used incorrect ones' — remember (s) for solids, (l) for liquids, (g) for gases, and (aq) for substances dissolved in water
Examiner Report 2023: 'Common error was not balancing equations properly' — always count atoms of each element on both sides and ensure they are equal before adding state symbols
note_data.keyDiagram: Students should be able to draw a step-by-step method flowchart showing: 1) Write word equation, 2) Replace words with chemical formulae, 3) Balance by adding coefficients (never change formulae), 4) Add state symbols in brackets. Include a visual example showing the progression from 'magnesium + oxygen → magnesium oxide' through to '2Mg(s) + O₂(g) → 2MgO(s)' with arrows between each step.
note_data.quickRecap: Matter can exist as compounds (elements chemically joined) or mixtures (substances not chemically joined). Pure substances have fixed properties while mixtures have variable properties. Different separation techniques are used based on the type of mixture: filtration for insoluble solids, crystallization to obtain solids from solution, distillation to obtain pure liquids, fractional distillation for miscible liquids, and chromatography for organic compounds.
